title: Projects
p1-descriptor:
Film and TV editors spend hours searching for temp music — sometimes just to score 20 seconds of a scene. SceneSync lets you drop in a reference track and get royalty‑free alternatives that match on an acoustic level, with a plain‑language explanation of why each one fits.

The core question: what in the audio signal produces that feeling? Not 'find something similar' — but 'here's the acoustic DNA of your reference, and here's why these suggestions share it.'

SceneSync extracts a 90‑value acoustic fingerprint via Meyda (energy, brightness, timbral texture, harmonic content, spectral properties) and runs cosine similarity search against a curated library of 243 royalty‑free tracks — 180 FMA tracks across 7 genres plus 63 Musopen classical pieces. Dimension weighting reduces production‑loudness bias so classical and pop tracks can surface as matches when they share musical character. An LLM explanation layer (via Vercel serverless proxy) translates the signal math into plain language — 'What you're hearing' for the reference track, 'Why this matches' for each suggestion. Audio analysis runs client‑side in a Web Worker; explanation calls go through a serverless function. Track history and feature vectors persist locally in IndexedDB, and match tracks stream via Cloudflare R2.

I started this as a scene classifier — predicting Action / Romance / Drama / Tension from audio features using TensorFlow.js. At 50% accuracy on 20 tracks, it was a coin flip, and more importantly, it wasn't answering the right question. A director doesn't want to hear 'this is a Drama track' — they want to know what in the audio makes it feel that way. The pivot to acoustic similarity search came from realizing the interesting problem wasn't prediction, it was explanation.

I've been obsessed with how music shapes scenes since I was a kid — the slapstick chaos of Tom and Jerry, the emotional precision of Studio Ghibli and Pixar scores, the way a sparse piano line can contradict what's on screen and make it land harder. These are the same patterns the Musicbed/Filmsupply 2026 Commercial Filmmaking Trend Report names as defining the industry right now: intentional contradiction between music and image, the quiet power of solo instrumentation, cross‑genre timbral exploration. SceneSync is built to surface and articulate those patterns — not just find similar tracks, but help editors understand why something works acoustically so they can make intentional choices instead of lucky ones.
p1-stack: React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Web Audio API, Web Workers, Meyda, Node.js, Python, IndexedDB, Cloudflare R2, Vercel Serverless Functions, DeepSeek API
p2-descriptor: In 2025, I led a 9-month digital transformation for Kuang-Hwa Vocational Senior High School — an institution my grandfather founded in 1951. The school needed a digital presence that matched its 70-year legacy and reflected its evolving mission. I project-managed a complete overhaul: migrating from a legacy system to WordPress/Elementor on Google Cloud Platform, coordinating with designer Genie's team, and working across departments (admissions, academics, alumni relations) to modernize how the school presents itself. The challenge wasn't just technical — it was navigating institutional culture, balancing stakeholder priorities, and building something sustainable for a team that would maintain it long after I stepped away. The site launched in September 2025. It addressed each department's needs and redefined what a school website could be—shifting from a passive information repository to an active tool for connecting the community.
p2-stack: WordPress, Elementor, Google Cloud Platform, Project Management
p3-descriptor: I built PollyGlot after trips to Italy and Spain where Duolingo failed me. I'd spent weeks learning phrases I'd never actually use in conversation — meanwhile, I couldn't figure out how to ask a local for restaurant recommendations without sounding either too formal or accidentally rude. Google Translate gives you words, but it doesn't help you navigate tone. PollyGlot lets you specify context before translating across 6 tones — formal, friendly, slang, and more. Need to ask a street vendor about their produce? Choose friendly. Writing an email to a professor? Formal. Trying to joke with your Airbnb host? Slang. It supports 16 languages including Klingon and Esperanto (because some experiments exist just for fun). The app uses OpenAI's API for translation and tone adjustment. It's not replacing human translators — cultural nuance is hard — but it bridges the gap between direct translation and actual conversation.
p3-stack: React, Node.js, Express, OpenAI API, CSS
p4-descriptor: Designed and built a single-page application where users can search for movies and nominate their top five favorites. Leveraged OMDB API to provide real-time movie data and React for an optimized interactive experience.
p4-stack: React, JavaScript, CSS, HTML, OMDB API
